names.scientific_name: Disodium diphosphate (E450i), Trisodium diphosphate (E450ii), Tetrasodium diphosphate (E450iii), Dipotassium diphosphate (E450v), Dicalcium diphosphate (E450vii)

natural_occurrence.description: Diphosphates do not occur naturally in free form. While phosphate compounds are essential components of all living organisms (DNA, ATP, bones), the diphosphate salts used in food are synthetically manufactured from phosphoric acid.

manufacturing.description: Produced by heating orthophosphates (monophosphates) to high temperatures (above 200°C), causing dehydration and condensation to form diphosphates (pyrophosphates). Different cation forms (sodium, potassium, calcium) are obtained by using the corresponding orthophosphate salts as starting materials.

other_uses.cosmetics: Buffering agent and chelating agent in toothpaste and dental products.
other_uses.industrial: Water treatment, metal surface treatment, detergents, textile processing.
other_uses.household: Toothpaste (tetrasodium pyrophosphate prevents tartar), cleaning products, baking powder.

eu.conditions: Quantum satis in many categories; specific limits apply due to concerns about total dietary phosphate intake.

usa.notes: Widely used in processed cheese and baking powder

japan.japanese_regulation_name: ピロリン酸ナトリウム

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0-70 mg/kg bw/day (as phosphorus, for all phosphates)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 40 mg/kg bw/day (as phosphorus, for all phosphates)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA's comprehensive evaluation of food phosphates expressed concern about high dietary intake from additives, particularly in Western processed food diets. Recommended monitoring phosphate levels in processed foods.

commonly_found_in: Processed cheese and cheese spreads (makes cheese melt smoothly)
fun_facts: Diphosphates are the secret ingredient that makes processed cheese melt smoothly and stay creamy - they prevent the proteins from clumping
The same chemical in your toothpaste preventing tartar (tetrasodium pyrophosphate) is also in your processed cheese
Sodium acid pyrophosphate in baking powder reacts slower than other acids, giving 'double-acting' leavening - once when wet, once when heated
Part of the reason canned tuna doesn't develop crunchy crystals (struvite) is thanks to diphosphates
Despite concerns about dietary phosphate overload in Western diets, phosphates are essential nutrients - your DNA and ATP (energy molecules) are built on phosphate backbones
